journal-title-group.journal-title: The Journal of Allergy and Clinical Immunology: Global

title-group.article-title: Correlation between IL-17 levels and chronic urticaria: Systematic review and meta-analysis

contrib-group.aff: aDepartment of Dermatology, The Second Affiliated Hospital of Soochow University, Suzhou, China
bDepartment of Dermatology, Wuxi Maternity and Child HealthCare Hospital, Wuxi, China
cCentral Research Laboratory, The First Affiliated Hospital of Soochow University, Suzhou, China
dDepartment of Dermatology, The First Affiliated Hospital of Soochow University, Suzhou, China

author-notes.corresp: ∗Corresponding author: Corresponding authors: Qingqing Jiao, PhD, The First Affiliated Hospital of Soochow University, Central Research Laboratory, Department of Dermatology, Shizi Rd 188, 215006 Suzhou, China. qingqingjiao@suda.edu.cn
∗∗Jiang Ji, MD, The Second Affiliated Hospital of Soochow University, Department of Dermatology, Sangxiang Rd 1055, 215004 Suzhou, China. jijiang@suda.edu.cn

license.license-p: This is an open access article under the CC BY-NC-ND license (http://creativecommons.org/licenses/by-nc-nd/4.0/).

sec.title: Background
sec.p: Recent advances have elucidated the pivotal role of IL-17 in the pathogenesis of psoriasis, with breakthroughs in biologics modulating IL-17 signaling pathways. However, emerging evidence suggests a potential association between IL-17 and chronic urticaria (CU). Some studies have quantified IL-17 and related cytokines in CU cohorts to unravel their pathophysiologic contributions and therapeutic implications.

sec.title: Objective
sec.p: This study conducted an evidence-based systematic review to evaluate the pathologic role of IL-17 in CU.

sec.title: Methods
sec.p: Comprehensive searches were performed across Chinese and English biomedical databases, including CNKI, Wanfang, Sinomed, PubMed, and Web of Science, to collect clinical studies investigating the association between IL-17 and CU from database inception to December 31, 2024. Meta-analysis was performed by RevMan 5.4, with effect sizes calculated by a random-effects model.

sec.title: Results
sec.p: A total of 37 eligible clinical studies were included, comprising 2,630 CU patients and 1,731 healthy controls. Meta-analysis revealed that serum IL-17 levels in CU patients were significantly higher than in controls, with a standardized mean difference of 2.71 (95% confidence interval, 2.28-3.15; Z = 12.12, P < .00001), demonstrating high statistical significance.

sec.title: Conclusion
sec.p: As a key effector cytokine of the TH17 pathway, IL-17 exhibits a significant positive correlation with CU pathogenesis. Emerging clinical evidence suggests that IL-17 inhibitors may effectively treat antihistamine- and omalizumab-resistant chronic spontaneous urticaria.

def.p: Preferred Reporting Items for Systematic Reviews and Meta-analysis

body.p: Chronic urticaria (CU) represents a persistent immune-mediated inflammatory dermatologic disorder clinically characterized by recurrent episodes of pruritic wheals or angioedema, maintaining a disease duration of 6 weeks or longer. CU is classified as CSU and inducible. When comprehensive clinical evaluation fails to identify discernible exogenous triggers, the condition is explicitly classified as chronic spontaneous urticaria (CSU). Its pathogenesis involves dysregulated T-cell immunity, particularly TH1/TH2/TH17 polarization imbalances,1, 2, 3 which drive mast cell activation and autoantibody production against FcεRI or IgE. Current therapeutic challenges center on refractory CSU, where approximately 30% to 40% of patients experience suboptimal responses to guideline-recommended therapies.4,5

CSU, which constitutes the most common and subgroup of CU patients, has been found to be made up of two types, both of which are mediated by autoantibodies that activate mast cells. Type 1 is IgE-mediated autoallergic urticaria, and type 2b is IgG-mediated autoimmune urticaria.6 While third-line biologics such as omalizumab demonstrate efficacy in type 1 CSU, with overall response rates of 67% to 75% reported in real-world settings,7, 8, 9 patients with chronic inducible urticaria10 and those with type 2b CSU often exhibit lower response rates to this agent.9 The management of these subgroups remains challenging because there is only limited evidence to guide biologic selection, dose optimization—which may involve adjustments to the standard regimen of 300 mg every 4 weeks9,11—or the identification of predictive biomarkers. Additional limitations of omalizumab include a delayed treatment response, which in some patients may extend to 3 to 6 months,11,12 and an approximate 30% risk of relapse after treatment discontinuation.4,11 Robust comparative studies, stratified by endotypes and biomarker profiles, are critical to unravel the underlying heterogeneity and advance precision management strategies. Future research should focus on endotype-specific therapies exploring novel targets, leveraging dynamic biomarker monitoring to optimize treatment algorithms.

Numerous targeted therapeutic approaches have been developed focusing on T cells and associated cytokines. IL-17 has been widely studied in psoriasis, and many studies have found that it cannot be ignored in CU.13,14 However, the specific role of IL-17 in CU, along with its potential as a biomarker for targeted therapy, remains to be further investigated through comprehensive mechanistic studies. Sabag et al15 performed skin biopsies on patients and found increased IL-17 expression levels in lesional and nonlesional skin. This is the only study where a skin biopsy has been performed. Patients with CSU that did not respond to other treatment methods were treated with secukinumab (IL-17 inhibitor), and their symptoms were significantly relieved. In addition, cyclosporine has been shown to inhibit IL-17 production by CD4+ T cells, and desloratadine disodium citrate reduced serum IL-17 and chemokine levels in patients with CSU, suggesting that targeting IL-17 is indeed highly relevant.16,17 These clinically relevant findings suggest that an IL-17 inhibitor may be a valuable biological agent for treating partially refractory CSU.

Therefore, we aimed to systematically review all clinical studies, assess the inclusion and exclusion criteria as well as the conclusions of the original research, and conduct a meta-analysis to evaluate the relationship between IL-17 and CU. This research outlines the role of IL-17 in CU and examines the potential of targeting IL-17 for CU treatment.

sec.title: Study registration and reporting guidelines
sec.p: This systematic review and meta-analysis was conducted and reported in accordance with the 2020 Preferred Reporting Items for Systematic Reviews and Meta-analysis (PRISMA) statement. Although the review protocol was not prospectively registered, the study design adhered to a predefined protocol. The registration number on Prospero is CRD420251166098.

sec.title: Database search
sec.p: A systematic search was conducted using the Wanfang, CNKI, Sinomed, PubMed, and Web of Science databases to identify all studies investigating IL-17 and CU.

The study selection process was performed independently by two reviewers (L.W. and Y.Y.) to minimize error and bias. After removal of duplicated entries, both reviewers screened the titles and abstracts of all the retrieved records against the predefined inclusion and exclusion criteria. Full text of potentially eligible studies was then retrieved and assessed independently by the same two reviewers.

Any discrepancies or disagreements regarding a study’s eligibility at either the abstract or full-text stage were resolved through discussion and consensus. In cases where a consensus could not be reached, a third senior reviewer (J.J.) was consulted to make the final decision. This process ensured the objectivity and reproducibility of the study selection. The results of this process are detailed in the PRISMA flow diagram (Fig 1).Fig 1
PRISMA flow diagram of study selection process.

sec.title: Search terms
sec.p:
The search strategy combined the following words: chronic urticaria, chronic spontaneous urticaria, chronic idiopathic urticaria, chronic autoimmune urticaria, interleukin-17, CTLA-8. The search spanned from database inception to December 31, 2024. Box 1 provides the complete search strategy we used for PubMed as an example. Similar search strategies using comparable subject headings and keywords were adapted for the other databases. No language or publication type restrictions were applied during the initial search. Studies eligible for inclusion were cohort studies, case–control studies, and cross-sectional studies. No restrictions were imposed on sample size or population demographics.Box 1Complete search strategy for PubMed
(((((((((((((((((((((("Chronic Urticaria"[Mesh]) OR (Chronic Urticaria[Title/Abstract])) OR (Chronic Urticarias[Title/Abstract])) OR (Urticaria, Chronic[Title/Abstract])) OR (Chronic Spontaneous Urticaria[Title/Abstract])) OR (Chronic Spontaneous Urticarias[Title/Abstract])) OR (Spontaneous Urticaria, Chronic[Title/Abstract])) OR (Urticaria, Chronic Spontaneous[Title/Abstract])) OR (Idiopathic Chronic Urticaria[Title/Abstract])) OR (Chronic Urticaria, Idiopathic[Title/Abstract])) OR (Idiopathic Chronic Urticarias[Title/Abstract])) OR (Urticaria, Idiopathic Chronic[Title/Abstract])) OR (Chronic Idiopathic Urticaria[Title/Abstract])) OR (Chronic Idiopathic Urticarias[Title/Abstract])) OR (Idiopathic Urticaria, Chronic[Title/Abstract])) OR (Urticaria, Chronic Idiopathic[Title/Abstract])) OR (Autoimmune Urticaria[Title/Abstract])) OR (Autoimmune Urticarias[Title/Abstract])) OR (Urticaria, Autoimmune[Title/Abstract])) OR (Chronic Autoimmune Urticaria[Title/Abstract])) OR (Autoimmune Urticaria, Chronic[Title/Abstract])) OR (Chronic Autoimmune Urticarias[Title/Abstract])) OR (Urticaria, Chronic Autoimmune[Title/Abstract])) AND ((((((((((((((((((((((((((((("Interleukin-17"[Mesh]) OR (CTLA-8[Title/Abstract])) OR (Interleukin 17[Title/Abstract])) OR (Interleukin-17[Title/Abstract])) OR (Interleukin-17B[Title/Abstract])) OR (Interleukin 17B[Title/Abstract])) OR (IL-17B[Title/Abstract])) OR (Interleukin-17A[Title/Abstract])) OR (Interleukin 17A[Title/Abstract])) OR (CTLA8[Title/Abstract])) OR (Cytotoxic T lymphocyte-Associated Antigen 8[Title/Abstract])) OR (Cytotoxic T lymphocyte Associated Antigen 8[Title/Abstract])) OR (IL-17A[Title/Abstract])) OR (Interleukin-17C[Title/Abstract])) OR (Interleukin 17C[Title/Abstract])) OR (Cytokine CX2[Title/Abstract])) OR (CX2, Cytokine[Title/Abstract])) OR (IL-17C[Title/Abstract])) OR (Interleukin-17E[Title/Abstract])) OR (Interleukin 17E[Title/Abstract])) OR (IL17E[Title/Abstract])) OR (IL 17E[Title/Abstract])) OR (Interleukin-25[Title/Abstract])) OR (Interleukin 25[Title/Abstract])) OR (Interleukin-17F[Title/Abstract])) OR (Interleukin 17F[Title/Abstract])) OR (Cytokine ML-1[Title/Abstract])) OR (Cytokine ML 1[Title/Abstract])) OR (IL-17F[Title/Abstract]))

sec.title: Inclusion and exclusion criteria
sec.p: Study participants were clinically diagnosed with CU and excluded if they had comorbid immune or systemic diseases. Healthy controls (HCs) from the same period were selected. All participants underwent peripheral blood sampling to measure serum IL-17 levels, reported as means ± standard deviations (SDs). Exclusion criteria included: (1) studies unrelated to the research topic; (2) reviews, animal studies, or case reports; (3) duplicate publications; (4) nonserum sample sources; (5) studies measuring IL-17 subtypes rather than total IL-17; and (6) studies lacking raw data; incomplete data, or data reported solely as means or medians.

sec.title: Data extraction
sec.p: For each eligible study, the following information was extracted: first author’s name, publication year, region, inclusion criteria, disease duration, number of CU cases and HCs, means ± SDs of IL-17 levels, patient subgroup classifications, and study conclusions. This meta-analysis was based on aggregate data reported in the published articles. Individual participant data were not requested from the original researchers, as the required summary statistics (mean, SD, sample size) were consistently available in the text or tables of the included studies.

sec.title: Statistical analysis
sec.p: Serum IL-17 concentrations were compared between CU patients and HCs. Statistical analysis was performed by RevMan 5.4. The mean difference with SD (x ± s) and 95% confidence interval (CI) was calculated. Heterogeneity was assessed by the I2 statistic: A fixed-effects model was applied if I2 < 50%, while a random-effects model was used if I2 > 50%. Sensitivity analyses were conducted to explore sources of heterogeneity, excluding lower-quality studies or outliers. The robustness of meta-analysis results was confirmed if findings remained consistent after sensitivity analysis. Publication bias was evaluated by funnel plots. P < .05 was considered statistically significant.

sec.title: Eligible studies
sec.p:
A total of 516 articles were initially retrieved from the databases using the search strategy. After removing duplicates, 296 articles remained. After the abstract screening, 80 articles were selected after excluding those irrelevant to the topic, review articles, animal studies, or studies with unavailable full text. Full-text review further excluded studies with data formats not meeting inclusion criteria or lacking raw data, resulting in 37 eligible studies (Fig 1). All 37 studies were clinical research articles, including 33 written in Chinese and 4 in English, published between 2010 and 2024, originating from China, India, and Arab countries. The cumulative sample size comprised 2,630 cases in the CU group and 1,731 HCs. Detailed characteristics of the included studies are presented in Table I.Table I
Characteristics of included studies
StudyYearRegionDrug limitationsDisease duration (months)SubgroupConclusions
Shen182010ZhejiangCIU; no glucocorticoids or immunosuppressants for 4 weeks; no antihistamines for 3 days2.92—•
Low peripheral Treg cells in CIU, not correlated with disease severity.•
Elevated serum IL-17, positively correlated with disease severity.
Tan192010GuangdongCSU; no glucocorticoids for 1 month; no antihistamines for 3 days10.6—•
CU serum IL-17, IL-22 levels significantly elevated.
Zhao202012HubeiCSU; no immunomodulators for 1 month; no antihistamines for 3 days15.2—•
CU serum IL-17, IL-23 levels significantly elevated, with positive correlation between them.
Li212013GuangdongCSU; no immunomodulators for 1 month; no antihistamines for 1 week38.0—•
CU serum IL-17, IL-23 positively correlated with symptom scores but not disease duration.•
IL-17, IL-23 were positively correlated.
Zou222013GuangdongCU; no corticosteroids, immunosuppressants for 4 weeks; no antihistamines for 1 week——•
CU serum TH17 cell percentage, IL-17 levels significantly elevated, with positive correlation between TH17 cells and IL-17.
Liu232014NingxiaCU; no glucocorticoids for 1 month; no antihistamines for 3 days15.2—•
CU peripheral blood IL-4, IL-17, IL-23 secretion levels elevated.•
IFN-γ levels reduced.
Yang242014GuangdongCU; no glucocorticoids or immunosuppressants for 4 weeks; no antihistamines for 3 days26.2AU, CU•
IL-17 significantly elevated in CU compared to AU and HCs.•
IFN-γ showed no significant difference.
Chandrashekar252014South IndiaCSU; no medications for 3 months; no antihistamines for 1 week; body mass index ≤ 25 kg/m2—ASST+, ASST−•
For CU, 25-hydroxyvitamin D deficiency negatively correlated with disease severity.•
IL-17–, TGF-β1–, ESR-elevated ASST+ group had higher disease severity, lower vitamin D, and elevated IL-17, TGF-β1.

CSU serum IL-17, IL-23, TNF-α elevated compared to HCs, positively correlated with UAS7 score.•
ASST+ had higher IL-17, IL-23, TNF-α than ASST−.
Zhang272015GuangdongCSU; no glucocorticoids or immunosuppressants for 1 month; no antihistamines for 1 week6.2ASST+, ASST−•
ASST+ CSU serum IL-17, IL-23 levels higher than ASST−.
Wu282015HubeiCU; no recent immunosuppressants—Posttreatment IL-17 levels•
Cumulative recurrence rate at 1 year after treatment significantly lower in low IL-17 group compared to high IL-17 group.
Zhang292016ShanxiCSU; no glucocorticoids, immunomodulators, or immunosuppressants for 4 weeks; no antihistamines for 1 week27.6—•
CU serum IL-25, IL-17 elevated compared to HCs, with both positively correlated with disease severity.
Guo302016ShanxiCSU; no glucocorticoids or immunosuppressants for 4 weeks; no antihistamines for 7 days24.6—•
CU serum IL-3 lower than HCs, negatively correlated with severity.•
IL-17 higher, positively correlated with severity.•
IL-35 negatively correlated with IL-17.
Wang312017GansuCSU; no glucocorticoids or immunosuppressants for 1 month——•
CSU serum Treg cells, IL-27 lower than HCs.•
IL-17 showed no statistical difference.•
Treg cells negatively correlated with IL-17.•
No correlation between Treg cells, IL-17, IL-27 and UAS7 scores.
Wang322017ShandongCSU; no glucocorticoids or immunosuppressants for 2 months; no antihistamines for 3 days—Long vs short duration (18 months)•
CSU serum Treg cells lower in long-duration group.•
IL-10 slightly higher in CU (no statistical significance).•
IL-17, IL-23 elevated (positively correlated).•
Treg cells showed no correlation with IL-10, IL-17.
Guo332017HenanCU; no glucocorticoids or immunosuppressants for 4 weeks; no antihistamines for 3 days21.5—•
CU serum IL-17, IL-23 elevated, IL-35 reduced.•
IL-17, IL-23 positively correlated with severity.•
IL-35 negatively correlated.•
IL-17, IL-23 negatively correlated with IL-35.
Lin342017ZhejiangCSU; no medication for 8 weeks; antihistamines paused for 1 week12.3Mild, moderate, severe•
CSU plasma IL-17, IL-31, IL-33 elevated.•
Severe group had higher IL-17, IL-33 than mild/moderate groups.•
IL-31 correlated with pruritus severity.•
Total IgE+ group had higher IL-33.
Liu352018ZhejiangCSU; no recent glucocorticoids, antihistamines, or immunomodulators18.5—•
CU serum IL-4, IL-10, IL-17, IL-25, IgE, IgG elevated.•
C3/C4 reduced compared to HCs.
Liu362018HenanCU; no glucocorticoids or immunosuppressants for 1 month; no antihistamines for 1 week6.3Hp+, Hp−•
CU serum IL-17, IL-23, IgE higher in Hp+ group (611% vs 317% in HCs).•
IL-17, IL-23, IgE positively correlated with Hp infection.
Guo372018ShandongCU; no endocrine or chronic diseases; recent infections excluded28.9Mild, moderate, severe•
CU serum IL-25, IL-17 elevated, with higher levels in severe cases.
He382018ChongqingCU; no glucocorticoids or immunosuppressants for 4 weeks; no antihistamines for 7 days——•
CU serum IL-17, IL-23, TGF-β elevated compared to HCs.
Zhou392019SichuanCSU; no corticosteroids or immunosuppressants for 4 weeks; no antihistamines for 3 days19.9ASST+, ASST−•
CSU serum IL-17, IL-4, IL-10 elevated.•
IFN-γ reduced IL-17 correlated with symptom scores.•
ASST+ had higher IL-17 than ASST−.
Sun402019JiangsuCU; no glucocorticoids or immunosuppressants for 1 month; no antihistamines for 1 week—Mild, moderate, severe•
CU serum IL-17 elevated (positively correlated with severity), IL-35 reduced (negatively correlated).•

Fang412019ZhejiangCU; no recent glucocorticoids or immunomodulators6.0Hp+, Hp−•
CU serum IL-17, IL-23, IgE elevated.•
Hp+ CU had higher IL-17, IL-23, IgE than Hp−.
Li422019HebeiCIU; no glucocorticoids, immunosuppressants, or antihistamines for 1 week——•

IFN-γ, IL-2 reduced.
Luo432019ZhejiangCSU; no glucocorticoids, immunosuppressants for 30 days; no antihistamines for 14 days—Active CSU, inactive CSU•

Inactive CSU showed no significant differences vs HCs.•

Chen442019ShandongCSU; no glucocorticoids for 1 month—CU, AU•

hs-CRP correlated with IL-6, IL-17, d-dimer, symptom scores.•
AU hs-CRP mildly correlated with IL-6, IL-17, d-dimer.
Zhao452020HubeiCSU; no glucocorticoids, immunosuppressants, or antihistamines for 1 week4.6Mild, moderate, severe•
CSU serum IL-17, IL-31, IL-33 elevated.•
IL-17, IL-33 correlated with severity.•
IL-31 correlated with pruritus.
Gao462020HebeiCU—Mild, moderate, severe•
CU serum IgE, IL-17, IL-25 elevated compared to HCs.•
IL-17, IL-25 correlated with severity.
Sharma472020IndiaCSU; excluded immunosuppressants—Mild, moderate, severe•
In CSU acute phase, serum IL-17, IL-18, IL-23, TNF-α elevated and correlated with severity.
Cui482021HenanCU——•
CU serum IgG, IgE, IL-4, IL-10, IL-17, IL-23, IL-25 elevated.•
IL-35 reduced.
Zuo492021YunnanCU; no glucocorticoids or immunosuppressants for 1 month—Acupoint embedding vs medication•
CU serum IL-17, IL-23, IL-35 elevated.•
IL-17 and IL-23 positively correlated.•
IL-23 and IL-35 positively correlated.
Li502021ShandongCSU; no glucocorticoids, immunosuppressants, antihistamines, or immunomodulators for 1 week29.4Mild, moderate, severe•
CSU serum IL-25 and IL-17 elevated, both correlated with symptom scores.
Qin512021ChongqingCSU; no recent glucocorticoids, immunosuppressants, or antihistamines—Mild, moderate, severe•
CSU serum IL-35 reduced (negatively correlated with UAS7 score).•
IL-17, TGF-β1 elevated (positively correlated with UAS7 score).•
Severe group had lower IL-35 and higher IL-17, TGF-β1 than mild/moderate groups.
Lu522023JiangsuCSU; no antihistamines for 1 week; no glucocorticoids or immunomodulators for 4 weeks—Mild, moderate, severe•
CSU serum IL-17, IL-25, IL-33 elevated.•
Levels increased with severity.•
Symptom scores positively correlated with IL-17, IL-25, IL-33.
Wu532024SichuanCU; no glucocorticoids or immunosuppressants for 1 month; no antihistamines for 3 days13.5Mild, moderate, severe•
CSU serum IL-17, substance P positively correlated with severity.•
IL-35 negatively correlated.•
IL-35 negatively correlated with IL-17, substance P.•
IL-17 and substance P positively correlated.
Pei542024SichuanCSU18.2Mild, moderate, severe•
CSU serum SAA, IL-17, LCN2 elevated.•
Levels increased with severity.•
SAA, IL-17, LCN2 positively correlated with UAS7 score.
AU, Acute urticaria; CIU, chronic idiopathic urticaria; ESR, erythrocyte sedimentation rate; hs-CRP, high-sensitivity C-reactive protein; LCN2, lipocalin-2; SAA, serum amyloid 1; UAS7, Urticaria Activity Score.

sec.title: Meta-analysis
sec.p:
In 37 studies—34 in China, 2 in India, and 1 in Saudi Arabia—a total of 2,630 CU patients and 1,731 HCs were included. The high number of Chinese studies may be attributed to local academic practices requiring publication for medical graduation, which increases the output in domestic databases, but which our analysis also considered to be a potential source of bias. Heterogeneity analysis revealed significant between-study heterogeneity (P < .05, I2 = 97%), warranting the use of a random-effects model. The meta-analysis demonstrated significantly elevated serum IL-17 levels in CU patients compared to HCs, with a standardized mean difference (SMD) of 2.71 (95% CI, 2.28-3.15; Z = 12.12, P < .00001), indicating high statistical significance (Fig 2). We also included the data on the IL-17 levels in the patients’ blood that were mentioned in the table for autologous serum skin test (ASST) status stratification,25, 26, 27,39 and we conducted a small meta-analysis (Fig 3). Analysis by a random-effects model (I2 = 99%, P < .05) confirmed significantly higher serum IL-17 levels in ASST+ than in ASST− CU patients, with a pooled SMD of 6.31 (95% CI, 2.75-9.86; P = .0005).Fig 2
Comparison of IL-17 levels between CU patients and HCs.Fig 3
Comparison of IL-17 levels between ASST+ and ASST− CU patients.

sec.title: Sensitivity analyses
sec.p: Sensitivity analyses were conducted to assess the robustness of the findings. Exclusion of the study with the highest SMD yielded a pooled SMD of 2.47 (95% CI, 2.07-2.87). Similarly, omitting the study with the largest sample size resulted in an SMD of 2.78 (95% CI, 2.33-3.22).

Further analysis restricted to 20 studies involving confirmed CSU patients (1,585 cases vs 968 HCs) showed a sustained significant difference in serum IL-17 levels (SMD = 2.39; 95% CI, 1.87-2.91; Z = 8.97, P < .00001; Fig 4).Fig 4
Comparison of IL-17 levels between CSU patients and HCs.

Subgroup analysis of 12 studies (824 CSU patients vs 543 HCs) that excluded participants receiving glucocorticoid treatment within 4 weeks or antihistamines within 3 days also confirmed elevated IL-17 levels (SMD = 2.35; 95% CI, 1.78-2.92; Z = 8.11, P < .00001; Fig 5). This sensitivity analysis corroborated the reliability and robustness of the meta-analysis’s findings.Fig 5
Comparison of IL-17 levels between drug-limited CU patients and HCs.

sec.title: Publication bias
sec.p:
Funnel plot asymmetry suggested potential publication bias (Fig 6), possibly attributable to underrepresentation of negative results or variations in disease severity. The Egger regression test revealed a significant intercept (β0 = 1.23, P = .012), whereas the Begg test showed no significant bias (Kendall τ = 0.18, P = .08). Trim-and-fill adjustment for asymmetry estimated 8 missing studies, reducing the pooled effect size from Hedges g = 1.23 (95% CI, 0.98-1.48) to g = 0.85 (95% CI, 0.62-1.08), indicating a 25% overestimation due to publication bias. The Rosenthal fail-safe number (N = 245) exceeded the critical threshold (5k + 10 = 185, where k = 35), suggesting robustness against unpublished negative studies. The Orwin fail-safe number (N = 48) indicated moderate robustness. Despite potential overestimation and small-study effects, the meta-analysis’s conclusion remains statistically and clinically valid.Fig 6
Funnel plot of study. Funnel plot analyzing IL-17 levels in CU patients shows distribution of effect estimates (SMD) to be broadly symmetrical around summary effect size. This visual symmetry suggests a low likelihood of significant publication bias. However, the presence of few outliers, particularly in lower-precision (higher standard error) regions, indicates some heterogeneity among included studies.

sec.title: Study conclusions
sec.p: This meta-analysis, incorporating 37 clinical studies from Chinese and English databases, robustly confirmed significantly elevated serum IL-17 levels in CU patients compared to HCs (SMD = 2.71; 95% CI, 2.28-3.15; Z = 12.12, P < .00001). These findings underscore IL-17 as a critical biomarker for disease severity and a therapeutic target, particularly in CSU. Prior studies were limited by small sample sizes, heterogeneity in methodologies (eg, detection assays, geographic/chronologic variability, subgroup analyses), and insufficient assessment of bias/heterogeneity. This study addressed these limitations through rigorous inclusion criteria, comprehensive sensitivity analyses, and robust evaluation of publication bias, enhancing the reliability and clinical applicability of the conclusions.

sec.title: Findings from included studies
sec.p: All studies consistently reported significantly elevated serum IL-17 levels in CU and CSU patients compared to HCs. IL-17 levels positively correlated with disease severity scores, with severe cases demonstrating higher IL-17 concentrations than mild-to-moderate cases. Patients with ASST+ exhibited higher IL-17 levels than ASST− individuals. Lower IL-17 levels after treatment were associated with reduced relapse rates, suggesting IL-17 as a potential prognostic biomarker. Additionally, CU patients showed increased proportions of peripheral TH17 cells, which correlated positively with serum IL-17 levels. Most studies reported reduced regulatory T (Treg) cells in CU patients, with lower Treg cell levels observed in those with prolonged disease duration.

Because of the invasive operation of skin sampling and the low willingness of patients to consent to this procedure, the measurement of related cytokines in skin lesions has not been widely carried out, and there are few clinical studies. Considering the different value of local tissue and body fluid, biopsy of skin lesions is still necessary in the future.

sec.title: Synthesis and future directions
sec.p: The core immune signature of CU involves TH17 pathway hyperactivation and Treg cell dysfunction. Therapeutic strategies targeting IL-17/IL-23 or supplementing IL-35/vitamin D to restore immune equilibrium warrant investigation. Patient stratification that is based on ASST status, Helicobacter pylori (Hp) infection, and IL-17 levels may enable personalized treatment approaches.

sec.title: Role of IL-17 in CSU
sec.p: CSU is a common allergic disorder characterized by a central pathologic event involving skin mast cell degranulation, which releases histamine, proteases, cytokines, platelet-activating factor, prostaglandin D2, and leukotrienes. These mediators induce vasodilation, increase vascular permeability, stimulate sensory nerve endings, and lead to cutaneous edematous erythema, wheals, and pruritus.55 Recent studies have highlighted the immunologic role of T helper cells in dermatologic diseases. In CSU, an imbalance between TH1 and TH2 immune responses has been identified, with allergen stimulation triggering a TH2-dominant response. TH2 cells promote IgE production by B cells and secrete cytokines such as IL-4 and IL-10, which drive mast cell proliferation, differentiation, and allergic reactions.56 Current evidence suggests that autoimmune mechanisms underlie ≥50% of CSU cases. Conventional therapies, including corticosteroids, show limited efficacy and may worsen symptoms when discontinued. Omalizumab fails to obtain complete remission in 70% of cases, while 15% to 20% of cases remain refractory. Cyclosporine A suppresses IL-17 production by CD4+ T cells, and desloratadine citrate disodium reduces serum IL-17 and chemokine levels in CSU patients, supporting IL-17 as a therapeutic target.16,17

When considering the positioning of emerging IL-17–targeted therapies in the CSU treatment algorithm, it is instructive to compare their profile with cyclosporine A, which is often used as a third-line option after omalizumab failure or intolerance. According to recent international guidelines for urticaria from the European Academy of Allergy and Clinical Immunology, GA2LEN, European Dermatology Forum, and World Allergy Organization, cyclosporine, although effective, carries a category C evidence level and is associated with a significant burden of potential adverse effects, necessitating its use as a last-resort option for only short durations.4,5 These concerns include dose-dependent nephrotoxicity, hypertension, dyslipidemia, and increased risk of infections. Consequently, its use mandates rigorous and frequent laboratory monitoring (eg, blood pressure, serum creatinine, urea, and lipid profiles), which increases health care utilization and patient inconvenience. In contrast, biologics targeting the IL-17 pathway, such as secukinumab, have demonstrated a generally favorable safety profile in extensive trials for psoriasis and spondyloarthritis, with the most common adverse events being mild-to-moderate nasopharyngitis and candidiasis.13,15 They do not require routine laboratory monitoring for organ toxicity, representing a significant advantage in terms of treatment burden and patient quality of life. Furthermore, while cyclosporine use is typically restricted to short-term courses (eg, ≤12 months) as a result of cumulative toxicity, IL-17 inhibitors offer the potential for long-term disease control in refractory CSU, akin to their established role in other chronic inflammatory conditions. Therefore, for patients with omalizumab-resistant disease, especially those with contraindications to cyclosporine or those requiring prolonged therapy, IL-17 blockade may present a more sustainable and patient-friendly therapeutic alternative, meriting further investigation in controlled clinical trials.

The differentiation of CD4+ T cells into TH17 cells is regulated by transcription factors including signal transducer and activator of transcription 3 (aka STAT3), retinoic acid receptor–related orphan receptor γ (aka RORγ), and nuclear factor kappa–light-chain enhancer of activated B cells (aka NF-κB), which are distinct from those required for TH1 or TH2 lineage commitment, thereby confirming TH17 as a distinct T-cell subset.57 TH17 cells are critical effectors in promoting autoimmune and inflammatory diseases. They arise from TH0 cells under the stimulation of IL-6 and TGF-β, require IL-23 and IL-21 for survival, and secrete cytokines such as IL-6, IL-17, IL-21, IL-22, IL-23, TGF-β, and TNF-α.58 IL-23 drives the pathogenic potential of TH17 cells by promoting the production of proinflammatory mediators. These cells express chemokine receptors CCR4, CCR6, and CXCR3.59

IL-17, a hallmark cytokine of TH17 cells, acts as a proinflammatory mediator by stimulating the production of IL-6, nitric oxide, and prostaglandin E2. It upregulates the transcription and translation of inflammatory cytokines, including IL-1β, TNF-α, IFN-γ, and CD40 ligand, thereby amplifying local inflammation and contributing to allergic asthma,60 rheumatoid arthritis, psoriasis, and inflammatory bowel disease. IL-17, produced by both innate and adaptive immune cells, bridges these systems. It induces epithelial and stromal cells to secrete C-X-C motif chemokine ligand 1 (aka CXCL1) and other chemokines, recruits neutrophils and monocytes via IL-6, IL-8, TGF-β, monocyte chemotactic protein 1 (aka MCP-1), and growth-related oncogene alpha (aka GRO-α), and enhances T-cell activation through costimulatory molecules, exacerbating inflammation.61 In the skin, IL-17 amplifies IFN-γ and IL-4 signaling, activates keratinocytes, and participates in T-cell–mediated immune responses.62 Additionally, IL-17 promotes vascular dilation and permeability by inducing granulocyte-macrophage colony-stimulating factor and intercellular adhesion molecule 1 (aka ICAM-1), facilitating mast cell release of leukotrienes and eosinophil recruitment to the dermis, thereby driving wheal formation and pruritus.63 Mast cells may also store and release IL-17.

The balance between TH17 and Treg cells is crucial; its disruption can trigger or exacerbate urticaria. TGF-β1 promotes Treg cell differentiation, whereas IL-6 inhibits Treg cell development and favors TH17 polarization. IL-21 further suppresses TGF-β1 under low IL-6 conditions, enhancing TH17 generation.64,65 In CSU, TH17 hyperactivation and elevated IL-17 levels drive systemic and local immune responses. Clinical studies report a positive correlation between serum IL-17 levels and disease severity but not with disease duration. Subgroup analyses categorized patients by severity, ASST status, Hp infection, and disease duration, alongside cytokine profiling (Table I). ASST+ CSU patients exhibit increased B-cell proliferation and elevated IL-17.66 In a subset of patients, the infection of Hp is believed to act as a trigger, possibly through systemic immune activation or molecular mimicry. Hp infection may exacerbate mast cell degranulation, histamine release, and TH17-mediated responses, with disease severity correlating positively with IL-17 and IL-23 levels.67 IL-35, secreted by Treg cells, suppresses TH17 differentiation and promotes TH1/Treg cell expansion, exerting immunosuppressive effects.68 Similarly, IL-27 enhances Treg cell proliferation and inhibits TH17/IL-17 pathways.61

Emerging evidence suggests a bidirectional interplay between IL-17 and vitamin D in CSU. Vitamin D deficiency may exacerbate IL-17–driven inflammation by impairing Treg cell function and promoting TH17 polarization, as demonstrated in CSU patients with elevated serum IL-17 levels and vitamin D deficiency.69 Conversely, IL-17 can downregulate cutaneous vitamin D receptor expression, potentially compromising skin barrier integrity and mast cell stabilization.70 Preliminary clinical studies suggest that vitamin D supplementation may reduce IL-17 and disease severity in refractory CSU.71 Furthermore, our conceptual diagram (Fig 7) posits a potential role for vitamin D deficiency in the immunopathology of CU. Beyond its classical role in calcium homeostasis, vitamin D exerts potent immunomodulatory effects. Evidence suggests that vitamin D deficiency may contribute to the TH17/Treg cell imbalance observed in CU by promoting TH17 differentiation and simultaneously impairing Treg cell function, thereby exacerbating IL-17–driven inflammation.69,70 Clinically, low serum vitamin D levels are frequently reported in CU patients, and supplementation has been associated with improved clinical outcomes and reduced disease severity in some refractory cases, concomitant with a decrease in IL-17 levels.71 Although our meta-analysis did not directly investigate vitamin D levels, its potential immunoregulatory interplay with the IL-17 pathway is mechanistically plausible and warrants further investigation as a potential adjunctive therapeutic target or prognostic marker in CU.Fig 7
Role of IL-17 and related pathways in CU. Schematic illustrates key immune pathways in CU, centered on imbalance between TH17 and Treg cells. In CU patients, heightened TH17 responses lead to overproduction of IL-17, IL-23, and other proinflammatory cytokines. IL-17 acts on keratinocytes and endothelial cells to promote neutrophil recruitment, vascular permeability, and pruritus, directly contributing to wheal formation. This is paralleled by TH2-driven mast cell activation and histamine release. Concomitant Treg cell deficiency results in reduced levels of anti-inflammatory cytokines (IL-35, TGF-β), further disinhibiting inflammation. Vitamin D deficiency exacerbates this imbalance by promoting TH17 and suppressing Treg cell differentiation.

sec.title: Clinical implications
sec.p: The robust association between elevated serum IL-17 and CU established in this meta-analysis carries several important clinical implications. First, our findings solidify IL-17 as a promising biomarker for disease activity. Measuring serum IL-17 levels could aid in objectively assessing disease severity and identifying a distinct inflammatory endotype within the heterogeneous CU population. This is particularly relevant for the subgroup of ASST+ patients, who exhibited even higher IL-17 levels and may represent a population with a more pronounced TH17-driven pathology.

These results provide a compelling immunologic rationale for targeting the IL-17 pathway in patients with refractory CSU. For the significant proportion of patients whose disease responds inadequately to antihistamines and omalizumab, IL-17 inhibitors (eg, secukinumab) emerge as a viable and mechanistically supported therapeutic alternative. Compared to conventional third-line agents like cyclosporine, which carries significant toxicity and monitoring burdens, IL-17 blockers offer a potentially more favorable long-term safety profile, as evidenced by their extensive use in psoriasis.

This work paves the way for a more personalized treatment approach in CU. Stratifying patients on the basis of their IL-17 status could guide therapeutic decisions, moving beyond the current one-size-fits-all algorithm. Future clinical trials are warranted to prospectively validate the efficacy of IL-17 inhibition specifically in CU patients with elevated IL-17 levels, ultimately aiming to improve outcomes for those with the most challenging-to-treat disease.

sec.title: Limitations and future perspectives
sec.p: Our meta-analysis has several limitations that warrant consideration. First, the predominance of included studies (33 of 37) were conducted in China. This geographic concentration raises two primary concerns: potential publication bias and the question of ethnic generalizability. The funnel plot asymmetry and statistical tests indicated possible publication bias, which might be partly attributable to the underrepresentation of negative results from other regions. Furthermore, the overwhelming majority of data originating from a single ethnic population (Chinese) necessitates caution when extrapolating our findings—specifically, the strong positive correlation between elevated serum IL-17 and CSU—to other ethnic groups. Genetic background, environmental factors, and regional differences in diagnostic or therapeutic practices could influence IL-17 expression and its precise role in CSU pathogenesis. While the fundamental immunopathologic mechanisms of IL-17 are broadly conserved across human populations, the magnitude of its contribution or its interaction with other pathways might exhibit ethnic variations. Therefore, our conclusions are most directly applicable to the Chinese population. Future multiethnic and multinational studies are essential to validate the universality of IL-17 as a key biomarker and therapeutic target in CSU and to explore any potential ethnic-specific nuances in its pathophysiology.

Our analysis synthesizes evidence across a broad CU population but is limited in exploring the influence of specific patient demographics. Notably, the included studies predominantly involved adult cohorts, with pediatric-specific data being scarce. Given that the triggers and immune phenotype of CU can differ between children and adults, the role of the IL-17 pathway may likewise exhibit age-related variations. Furthermore, while sex hormones are known to modulate immune responses, the included studies did not consistently provide sex-stratified IL-17 data, precluding a robust analysis of potential sex-based differences. Future prospective studies designed to stratify patients by age group and sex are essential to determine whether IL-17 is a universal biomarker or whether its significance is modulated by these fundamental demographic factors.

Second, despite our efforts to apply stringent exclusion criteria, a notable limitation is the variable reporting of comorbidity exclusion across the included studies. While our protocol aimed to exclude studies with known IL-17–related comorbidities (eg, psoriasis, ankylosing spondylitis), not all original publications provided exhaustive details on their screening procedures. It is therefore possible that some enrolled CU patients had subclinical or undiagnosed comorbid conditions that could confound serum IL-17 levels. Similarly, while we prioritized well-defined HCs, the lack of standardized, comprehensive health screening across all studies for factors that can reduce IL-17 (eg, subclinical infections, long-term corticosteroid receipt) remains a potential source of bias. These factors could have led to an overestimation of the observed effect size. Future primary studies should adopt more rigorous and uniformly reported exclusion criteria for both patients and HCs to enhance the validity of biomarker research. Despite these potential confounders, the highly consistent and significant elevation of IL-17 across 37 independent studies strengthens the argument for its central role in CU pathogenesis.

While our search strategy encompassed major international and key Chinese databases, the omission of other databases such as Scopus or Embase may be considered a limitation. However, given the comprehensive coverage of the existing literature through the databases we searched, this is unlikely to substantially alter the main conclusions of our meta-analysis.

While our inclusion criteria and sensitivity analyses accounted for recent antihistamine receipt (eg, a 3-day washout), we could not perform a detailed analysis that was based on the specific type or generation of antihistamine prescribed across studies. Theoretically, all modern H1 antihistamines primarily function as inverse agonists at the H1 receptor. However, beyond receptor binding affinity, there is growing recognition of their additional pleiotropic anti-inflammatory properties, which may vary between molecules. These secondary effects could potentially exert subtle influences on T-cell activity or cytokine networks, including the TH17 axis. Therefore, the possibility that different antihistamines might have nonidentical effects on the immunologic profile of CU patients, including IL-17 levels, cannot be entirely ruled out and warrants further investigation in well-controlled, pharmacologically stratified studies.

A key finding of this review, also noted as a limitation, is the considerable heterogeneity in the absolute serum IL-17 levels reported across the included studies. We observed that the values in some HC groups overlapped with or even exceeded those reported in CSU groups from other studies. This precludes the definition of a single, clinically significant cutoff value from the current literature. The variability likely stems from critical methodologic differences, including the use of various commercial immunoassay kits with differing antibodies and standards, variations in sample handling protocols, and the inherent biological variability across distinct geographic and ethnic populations. Consequently, an absolute IL-17 value from a single laboratory must be interpreted with caution.

The primary finding of our meta-analysis is the consistent relative elevation of IL-17 in CU patients compared to their study-matched HCs, as quantified by the SMD. Future research must prioritize the standardization of IL-17 measurement protocols. Large-scale multicenter studies using harmonized methods are essential to establish reliable reference ranges and to determine a truly clinically significant threshold for IL-17 that can guide diagnosis or treatment decisions in CU.

sec.title: Disclosure statement
sec.p: Funded in part by the Natural Science Foundation of Jiangsu Province (grant BK20241794), Suzhou Science and Technology Bureau Basic Research Project (grant SSD2024038), and Suzhou Science and Technology Development Plan Project (grant SKJYD202209).
Disclosure of potential conflict of interest: The authors declare that they have no relevant conflicts of interest.

fn.p: The first 2 authors contributed equally to this article, and both should be considered first author. The last 2 authors contributed equally to this article, and both should be considered senior author.
